ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Parmigiana di Melanzane as a prepared dish. FOODON_00002809 (eggplant food product) is used as the closest parent. A more specific prepared-dish term is absent from the ontology.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00GJ (aubergines/eggplants, cooked) is the closest defensible parent. No specific FoodEx2 code exists for the composite baked dish with cheese and tomato sauce.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for Parmigiana di Melanzane as a prepared food item.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of Parmigiana di Melanzane is approximately 300 g, comprising several layered slices of baked eggplant with tomato sauce and melted cheese.

nutrient_source.title: Eggplant Parmesan (Parmigiana di Melanzane) — composite nutrient estimate based on USDA FoodData Central ingredient data
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central (Foundation Foods and SR Legacy)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single USDA FoodData Central entry exists for the complete prepared dish Parmigiana di Melanzane. Nutrient values are estimated from a weighted composite of USDA SR Legacy entries for the primary ingredients: cooked eggplant (FDC ID 169228), canned tomato sauce (FDC ID 170457), whole-milk mozzarella cheese (FDC ID 173420), Parmesan cheese (FDC ID 171241), and olive oil (FDC ID 171413). Proportions are based on a standard recipe yielding approximately 100 g per portion unit: eggplant ~45%, tomato sauce ~25%, mozzarella ~18%, Parmesan ~7%, olive oil ~5%. Values represent a reasonable mid-range estimate for a restaurant-style preparation.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; supplemented by University of Sydney GI Database analogous estimate for mixed vegetable-cheese baked dish
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Parmigiana di Melanzane in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI is estimated at 35 based on analogous low-GI baked vegetable dishes with cheese and tomato sauce. Eggplant has a very low GI (approximately 15), tomato sauce has a low GI (approximately 35), and mozzarella/Parmesan contribute negligible glycemic load. The composite dish GI is estimated at 35 (low category, ≤55). Glycemic load per 300 g serving = (35 × 8.5 × 3) / 100 = 8.9 g. This is consistent with published estimates for similar Italian baked vegetable dishes in Atkinson et al. (2021), Am J Clin Nutr.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from cheese and tomato sauce
hypertension.guidance: A 300 g serving provides approximately 960 mg sodium, which represents 40–48% of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2000–2400 mg; individuals with hypertension should monitor portion size and request reduced-salt preparation where possible.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index and moderate carbohydrate content
type2_diabetes.guidance: With a GI of approximately 35 and a glycemic load of 8.9 per standard serving, Parmigiana di Melanzane is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed in standard portions, as it produces a modest postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from mozzarella and Parmesan cheese
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: A 300 g serving provides approximately 11.4 g saturated fat, which is 57% of the 20 g daily limit recommended for cardiovascular risk reduction; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit portion frequency and consider lower-fat cheese alternatives.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Parmigiana di Melanzane, also known as Eggplant Parmigiana or Aubergine Parmesan, is a classic Southern Italian baked dish originating from the Campania and Sicily regions of Italy. It is prepared by layering sliced eggplant (aubergine) that has been salted, drained, and either fried or baked, with a rich tomato sauce, slices of buffalo mozzarella cheese, grated Parmigiano-Reggiano, and fresh basil leaves. The assembled layers are baked in the oven until the cheese is melted and golden and the eggplant is tender throughout. The dish has a soft, yielding texture with a savoury, slightly tangy flavour from the tomato sauce balanced by the creamy richness of the mozzarella and the sharp, salty notes of the Parmesan. It is inherently vegetarian and gluten-free in its traditional form. Nutritionally, it provides moderate energy (approximately 118 kcal per 100 g), meaningful protein from the dairy components (5.8 g per 100 g), and dietary fibre from the eggplant (2.1 g per 100 g). The dish has a low glycemic index of approximately 35, making it suitable for individuals managing blood glucose levels. However, the sodium content from the cheeses and tomato sauce (approximately 320 mg per 100 g) warrants attention for individuals with hypertension or cardiovascular disease. Calcium content is notable at approximately 145 mg per 100 g due to the mozzarella and Parmesan. In Singapore, this dish is served at Italian restaurants such as Ricciotti and is typically priced as a main course.

confidence_reason: No single approved primary source entry exists for the complete prepared dish Parmigiana di Melanzane. Nutrient values are estimated from a weighted composite of USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy ingredient entries using a standard recipe proportion model. GI is an analogous estimate based on the University of Sydney GI Database for comparable low-GI baked vegetable dishes. Confidence is medium rather than low because the ingredient-level USDA data is high quality and the recipe proportions are well-established for this traditional dish.
notes: 1. Food group corrected from input value 'Grains and staples' to 'Vegetables' as Parmigiana di Melanzane is a baked vegetable dish; eggplant is the primary ingredient. 2. Nutrient values are composite estimates from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy ingredient data; no single prepared-dish entry was found in any approved primary source. 3. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not meaningfully reported for this dish in the USDA source data and is not a significant contributor in this food. 4. Trans fat is reported as 0.1 g per 100 g reflecting trace naturally occurring trans fats from dairy (conjugated linoleic acid); this is not an industrially added trans fat. 5. GI of 35 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for Parmigiana di Melanzane was found in the University of Sydney GI Database. 6. The dish is vegetarian (V) as indicated in the input record; it contains dairy and is not vegan. 7. Halal status is not confirmed as buffalo mozzarella sourcing and preparation method at the specific restaurant are unknown. 8. Cuisine tags in the input ('Pasta', 'Pizza', 'Chicken Dish') appear to be platform-level restaurant category tags rather than dish-specific tags and have not been carried forward as food classification attributes. 9. Monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are estimated from USDA ingredient composite and are approximate. Vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA source data for this dish composition.
